findings.summary: Task/data helpers mix throw, null-return, and swallow-on-error contracts
evidence: `useSegmentOutputsForShot` logs with `handleError(..., showToast: false)` and then rethrows (`throw error`) in multiple query functions.
`fetchTask` in `taskDataService` logs and returns `null` on both query errors and catch-path failures.
`ensureShotParentGenerationId` logs with `handleError` and then throws a new wrapped `Error`.
`submitSegmentTask` metadata fetch/update failures are logged then early-returned, while task creation failures throw.
findings.suggestion: Standardize one failure contract for this layer. For example: make service/task helpers return `Result<T, AppError>` (or `{ ok, data, error }`) and never throw; only boundary hooks that integrate with React Query throw. Refactor `fetchTask`, `ensureShotParentGenerationId`, and `submitSegmentTask` helper paths to that shared contract and document non-fatal metadata-save failures explicitly.
findings.reasoning: Inconsistent contracts force callers to guess whether to handle `null`, catch exceptions, or ignore silent fallbacks, which increases missed error branches and inconsistent retry/UX behavior.

findings.summary: Several browser/media error paths suppress failures without shared telemetry
evidence: `InlineEditVideoView` suppresses `video.play()` failures with `.catch(() => {})` in multiple handlers.
`StrokeOverlay` uses empty `catch {}` blocks for pointer capture/release failures.
`getStoredPromptCount` has a bare `catch {}` and silently falls back to `1`.
`upload.ts` duration probe resolves `0` on video element error without any error reporting path.
findings.suggestion: Add a shared non-fatal reporting helper (e.g., `reportNonFatalError(context, error, { showToast: false, fallback })`) that routes through `handleError` with structured `logData`. Replace empty catches and silent `resolve(0)` fallbacks with this helper so fallback behavior is preserved but failures stay observable.
findings.reasoning: Current silent suppression hides recurring runtime issues and makes fallback sentinel values indistinguishable from legitimate data, reducing debuggability and consistency.

findings.summary: UI-facing error payloads drift between `Error` objects and plain strings
evidence: `TaskDetailsData` models error as `Error | null`.
`ApplyResult` models error as optional `string`.
`PaymentFailureView` consumes `errorMessage: string`.
`SharePageError` consumes `message: string`.
findings.suggestion: Define a shared UI error contract (for example `DisplayError { message: string; code?: string; cause?: unknown }`) plus a `toDisplayError(error: unknown)` normalizer at module boundaries. Update `ApplyResult`, `TaskDetailsData`, and page error props to consume this single type.
findings.reasoning: Mixed error types across adjacent UI flows cause repetitive ad hoc conversions and inconsistent loss of error metadata (codes/causes), making user messaging and analytics harder to standardize.

findings.summary: Utility ownership is split across generic utils entrypoints.
evidence: src/shared/lib/utils.ts is a shared facade that re-exports multiple helper domains from one import path.
The packet import graph marks src/shared/lib/utils.ts as a high-gravity dependency (142 importers).
src/shared/components/JoinClipsSettingsForm/utils.ts is domain-specific math but uses the same generic utils naming pattern.
findings.suggestion: Rename src/shared/components/JoinClipsSettingsForm/utils.ts to frameQuantization.ts and keep exports domain-named. In parallel, deprecate or narrow src/shared/lib/utils.ts so imports target explicit modules (uiClassNames, stringFormatting, timeFormatting) rather than a catch-all alias.
findings.reasoning: When utility ownership is ambiguous, helpers tend to accrete in whichever utils file is most convenient, which weakens cohesion and increases dependency gravity.

findings.summary: VideoLightbox combines API contract exports with orchestration wiring.
evidence: VideoLightbox.tsx defines broad prop contracts (including grouped and nested props) and also re-exports controller model types.
The same VideoLightbox.tsx module acts as runtime composition glue across mode/environment/state/edit/render hooks and layout providers.
JoinClipsSettingsForm/utils.ts shows a narrower abstraction style: small, focused functions for one domain concern.
findings.suggestion: Extract VideoLightboxProps, VideoLightboxVideoProps, and exported controller model types into a dedicated contracts file (for example VideoLightbox.contracts.ts), then keep VideoLightbox.tsx focused on runtime composition only.
findings.reasoning: Separating stable contracts from volatile orchestration improves abstraction fitness by clarifying dependency direction and reducing coupling at the component entrypoint.

findings.summary: Comment-heavy helper files restate obvious code paths and dilute signal.
evidence: Packet `ai_debt_signals` shows comment_ratio around 0.30-0.54 for these files, while codebase_avg_comment_ratio is 0.067.
`useDeleteGenerationWithConfirm` and `useUpdatingTimestamp` include long explanatory/example blocks around thin wrappers.
`getImageAspectRatioStyle`, `camelToSnakeKeys`, and storage path helpers contain step-by-step comments that mostly mirror code behavior.
findings.suggestion: Reduce docs to intent/constraints only: keep one short WHY comment per function, remove restating HOW comments and long examples for trivial wrappers, and prefer concise type names/tests for behavior documentation.
findings.reasoning: High prose-to-code ratio is a common AI-generated artifact that increases maintenance cost and makes real behavioral changes harder to spot in review.

findings.summary: Deprecated compatibility layers remain active with date comments but weak removal enforcement.
evidence: `src/types/shots.ts` and `legacyStructureVideo.ts` both carry sunset contracts with `Remove by: 2026-05-31` but remain active compatibility entrypoints.
Packet import graph shows `src/types/shots.ts` is still highly coupled (203 importers), indicating migration completion is not yet enforced.
`ImageGenerationForm/types.ts` still exposes multiple `@deprecated` fields, expanding legacy surface area beyond a single migration boundary.
findings.suggestion: Enforce sunset in tooling: add `no-restricted-imports` for legacy facades (`@/types/shots`, `useShots` shim), add a CI check that fails on new legacy imports, and isolate deprecated fields behind one dedicated migration adapter to shrink the public type surface.
findings.reasoning: Comment-only deprecation contracts tend to drift; without hard gates, compatibility shims become permanent and accumulate AI-style migration debt.
